coredata.dc:description: Multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacterial infections, typified by methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), have become a severe global challenge to healthcare systems. In response, antibacterial carbon dots (CDs) have attracted growing interest in the fight against MRSA. In this study, nitrogen self-doped ginkgo leaf (GL) carbon dots (GLCDs) were synthesized through a facile one-step solvothermal method combined with extraction technology. The prepared GLCDs are spherical nanoparticles with a crystalline structure and carry a positive charge of + 7.62 mV. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of GLCDs against MRSA was found to be 250 μg/mL. Notably, at a concentration of 750 μg/mL, GLCDs could completely eliminate 1 × 106 MRSA within 240 minutes and effectively suppress MRSA biofilm formation in a dose-dependent manner. The antimicrobial mechanisms involve electrostatic interaction enabling penetration into MRSA, disruption of the cell structure leading to intracellular component leakage, induction of oxidative stress, and inhibition of antioxidant enzymes and respiratory function. Moreover, GLCDs demonstrated excellent biocompatibility in hemolysis and in vitro cytotoxicity tests. This research presents a novel natural-plant-based antimicrobial nanomaterial, providing valuable insights into the rational utilization of natural biomass to combat MRSA infections.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacterial infections, typified by methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), have become a severe global challenge to healthcare systems. In response, antibacterial carbon dots (CDs) have attracted growing interest in the fight against MRSA. In this study, nitrogen self-doped ginkgo leaf (GL) carbon dots (GLCDs) were synthesized through a facile one-step solvothermal method combined with extraction technology. The prepared GLCDs are spherical nanoparticles with a crystalline structure and carry a positive charge of + 7.62 mV. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of GLCDs against MRSA was found to be 250 μg/mL. Notably, at a concentration of 750 μg/mL, GLCDs could completely eliminate 1 × 106 MRSA within 240 minutes and effectively suppress MRSA biofilm formation in a dose-dependent manner. The antimicrobial mechanisms involve electrostatic interaction enabling penetration into MRSA, disruption of the cell structure leading to intracellular component leakage, induction of oxidative stress, and inhibition of antioxidant enzymes and respiratory function. Moreover, GLCDs demonstrated excellent biocompatibility in hemolysis and in vitro cytotoxicity tests. This research presents a novel natural-plant-based antimicrobial nanomaterial, providing valuable insights into the rational utilization of natural biomass to combat MRSA infections.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections have long posed a persistent and formidable challenge to the global healthcare infrastructure [1]. In recent years, the rapid emergence and dissemination of MDR bacteria have exacerbated this situation, elevating it to a full-scale global emergency [2]. Epidemiological data from the United States reveals that MDR bacteria are responsible for approximately 3 million infections annually, resulting in around 48,000 deaths [3]. This places an enormous strain on healthcare systems, with the estimated per-patient treatment cost reaching up to $50,000 [4]. This cost encompasses not only direct expenses for medications and hospital stays but also indirect costs related to lost productivity and long-term care [5]. Biofilm formation represents a significant hurdle in treating bacterial infections [6]. Biofilms are intricate, structured bacterial communities encased in a self-produced extracellular matrix [7]. Alarmingly, they are implicated in up to 80 % of clinical infections [8]. The biofilm matrix acts as a physical barrier, impeding the penetration of antibiotics and immune cells [9]. As a result, the efficacy of traditional antibiotic therapies is severely compromised, prolonging treatment duration and increasing the risk of treatment failure and infection recurrence [10]. Thus, there is an urgent need for novel and effective alternatives to traditional antimicrobial agents [11].

CDs, a unique class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have emerged as a promising solution in the battle against bacterial infections [12]. Their distinctive optical properties, such as fluorescence tunability, combined with their ease of modification and functionalization, nanoscale dimensions, excellent water dispersibility, favorable biocompatibility, and simple synthesis methods, render them highly versatile for antibacterial applications [13]. Regarding their antibacterial mechanisms, a growing body of research has elucidated multiple pathways [14]. For example, as previously reported, some CDs can disrupt the bacterial cell membrane through electrostatic interaction [15]. This leads to the leakage of intracellular components, as observed through high-resolution microscopy and biochemical assays, ultimately causing cell death [16]. Moreover, it demonstrated that certain CDs can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) [17]. These ROS can oxidize essential biomolecules within the bacteria, including proteins and nucleic acids, as determined by spectroscopic analysis and proteomic studies, thereby inhibiting bacterial growth [18]. Additionally, surface-functionalized CDs can specifically target certain bacterial strains, enhancing their antibacterial efficiency [19]. However, CDs synthesized using chemical reagents, although showing antibacterial potential, face significant obstacles [20]. Their raw materials often pose environmental and health risks [21]. What’s more, the high costs associated with their production, mainly due to expensive reagents and complex purification processes as reported in cost-analysis studies, have limited their large-scale application [22]. This has spurred researchers to explore more cost-effective and environmentally sustainable raw materials for CDs synthesis [23].

Against this backdrop, this study utilized natural bio-waste ginkgo leaves as a carbon source. Ginkgo leaves are abundant, renewable, and environmentally friendly. Employing them as a starting material not only reduces waste but also contributes to the development of sustainable nanomaterials. Through a straightforward one-step hydrothermal process and extraction technology, eschewing organic reagents, nitrogen self-doped antibacterial GLCDs were successfully synthesized. GLCDs outperform traditional antibiotics against MRSA and show comparable efficacy to chemically-made CDs, positioning them as a viable alternative. They powerfully inhibit MRSA biofilm formation by disrupting initial attachment and matrix development, crucial for treating challenging biofilm-associated infections. As biomass-sourced CDs, GLCDs exhibit high biocompatibility. Cytotoxicity assays on mouse embryonic cells (3T3) and red blood cells (RBCs) show minimal toxicity, and hemolysis assays reveal no significant red blood cell lysis. This research unlocks new avenues for applying plant-based CDs in antimicrobial therapeutics, potentially combating MDR bacteria and improving public health.

The initial phase involves the procurement of Ginkgo biloba leaves from the premises of Anhui Science and Technology University for use as a carbon source. Chemical reagents, including sodium chloride, monopotassium phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, potassium chloride, and ortho-nitrophenyl-β-D-galactopyranoside (ONPG), were procured from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., LTD (Nanjing, China). The strain of MRSA (ATCC 43300) was obtained from Hangzhou Baosai Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Hangzhou, China). The ‌Luria-Bertani (LB), tryptic soy broth (TSB) and Mueller-Hinton (MH) broth media were sourced from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All culture media specific to 3T3, such as penicillin-streptomycin, were acquired from Gibco BRL (Grand Island, NY, USA). The SYTO Green-Fluorescent Nucleic Acid Stains assay kit was bought from Molecular Probes (Eugene, OR, USA). The B-PER bacterial protein extraction reagent (a bacterial lysis agent) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (Waltham, MA, USA). Quantification of cytotoxicity was carried out using a live/dead viability/cytotoxicity kit (Invitrogen, CA, USA). The Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) and 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was obtained from Dojindo, Kumamoto. In all experimental procedures, ultrapure water (18 MΩ) was utilized. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS; formulated with 10 mM Na₂HPO₄, 2.0 mM KH₂PO₄, 137 mM NaCl, and 5.0 mM KCl; pH 7.4) was employed to simulate physiological conditions.

Ginkgo biloba leaves are highly appropriate for the production of CDs due to their substantial carbon content and favorable bioavailability [24]. Initially, 10 g of dried Ginkgo biloba leaves are pulverized by a small grinder. The resulting mixture was combined with 50 mL of petroleum ether to form a slurry, which was then subjected to ultrasonic-assisted extraction in the same solvent at ambient temperature for 30 minutes. Thereafter, the extract is heated in a sealed stainless steel reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene at 180 °C for 3 hours. After that, the solution from the hydrothermal reaction is filtered, generating a brown filtrate. This filtrate is then centrifuged at a rotational speed of 12,000 revolutions per minute for 20 minutes. Subsequently, the centrifuged supernatant is extracted with water. Then, the aqueous layer of the extraction is transferred into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 500 Da, and dialyzed against distilled water for 24 hours, with the distilled water replaced every 6 hours. Finally, the dialyzed carbon dot solution is filtered through a 220 nm filter membrane and dried via freeze-drying equipment, thus yielding the final CDs product.

The researcher uses the JEOL JEM-2100F (from JEOL Ltd., Japan) to obtain high-resolution images and diffraction patterns of GLCDs. The sample is prepared by dropping a GLCDs solution on a carbon-coated grid and dried for analysis. An operator employs the Bruker D8 ADVANCE (by Bruker AXS GmbH, Germany) to irradiate GLCDs powder on a silicon wafer and record the diffraction pattern within 10–80° 2θ range to study its crystal structure. The analyst utilizes the Nicolet iS50 (from Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) to measure the absorption spectra of a GLCDs-KBr pellet in 400–4000 cm−1 range to identify its functional groups. A scientist operates the Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (by Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) to irradiate GLCDs powder and analyze the elemental composition and chemical states of C, N, and O in GLCDs. The technician uses the Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS (from Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) to measure the electrophoretic mobility of diluted GLCDs solution in a cuvette to calculate its zeta potential. Ultraviolet-Visible Absorption Spectroscopy (UV-Vis) (Test Range: 200–800 nm) The experimenter uses the Shimadzu UV-2600 (by Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) to measure the absorbance of GLCDs solution in a cuvette over 200–800 nm to study its electronic transitions. The researcher uses the Edinburgh Instruments FLS980 (from Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK) to record the emission spectra of GLCDs solution in a cuvette with excitation wavelengths varying from 300 to 500 nm at 20 nm intervals. The investigator uses the Hitachi F-7000 (by Hitachi High-Technologies Corporation, Japan) to scan GLCDs solution in a cuvette and obtain a 3D fluorescence spectrum within 200–700 nm excitation and emission range to understand its fluorescence properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methodology for determining the MIC of GLCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To detect the MICs of GLCDs and vancomycin against MRSA via the turbidimetric method [25], first, inoculate MRSA into TSB, incubate at 37 °C with shaking (200 rpm) to the mid-logarithmic phase and adjust its concentration to about 1 × 106 CFU/mL. Then, prepare a stock solution of GLCDs and conduct serial dilutions, and also dilute the vancomycin solution appropriately. Next, add 100 μL of the MRSA suspension to each well of a 96-well microplate and then add 100 μL of the diluted GLCDs or vancomycin solutions in triplicate, along with positive and negative control wells. After incubating at 37 °C for 18–24 h, measure the OD at 600 nm. The MIC is the lowest concentration where the OD is similar to or lower than the negative control, allowing for the evaluation and comparison of the antibacterial activities of GLCDs and vancomycin against MRSA, providing crucial insights into their potential applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental method for the bactericidal kinetics of GLCDs against MRSA
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental procedures for investigating the bactericidal kinetics of GLCDs against MRSA encompass a series of crucial steps [26]. To begin with, a pure strain of MRSA is procured and introduced into MH broth. This culture is then incubated under 37 °C with shaking at 200 rpm until it reaches the mid-logarithmic growth phase. Subsequently, the bacteria are collected via centrifugation (5000 rpm for 10 min) and resuspended in PBS (pH 7.4), thereby generating a standardized bacterial suspension. Moving on, the GLCDs powder was measured and dissolved in an appropriate solvent. Utilizing serial dilution techniques, a range of GLCDs solutions with varying concentrations are prepared. Next, equal aliquots of the bacterial suspension (1 × 106 CFU/mL) are distributed into sterile tubes. The prepared GLCDs solutions are added to attain the intended final concentrations (250, 500, 750 μg/mL), and the mixtures are incubated at 37 °C with gentle shaking to mimic physiological circumstances. At predetermined time intervals, small samples are extracted from the reaction mixtures, and the bactericidal activity is promptly halted. Thereafter, the quenched samples are serially diluted in sterile diluents and plated onto agar plates. Following incubation, the colony-forming units (CFUs) are counted. In the final stage, the logarithm of the CFU counts is computed, kinetic curves are plotted against time for each GLCDs concentration, and essential parameters such as the killing rate, lag phase, and overall efficacy of GLCDs against MRSA are analyzed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ONPG assay of GLCDs against MRSA
ce:section.ce:para: β-galactosidase is a key enzyme involved in lactose metabolism in the cytoplasm of bacteria and plays a crucial role in maintaining the energy source of bacteria. Detection of intracellular β-galactosidase activity can reflect changes in cell membrane permeability. ONPG can react with β-galactosidase to produce o-nitrophenol. The effect of GLCDs on β-galactosidase activity of MRSA can be indirectly reflected by detecting the absorbance value of o-nitrophenol at 420 nm [27]. Briefly, 500 μL of MRSA (3 × 107 CFU/mL) culture were mixed with 500 μL GLCDs solutions (1000 μg/mL) and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. 500 μL of ONPG (6 mg/mL) solution were then added to initiate the reaction. Absorbance at 420 nm was recorded every 5 min for 2 h to quantify o-nitrophenol production. Data were plotted to analyze β-galactosidase activity inhibition by GLCDs, reflecting membrane permeability changes and bactericidal mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental method for detecting nucleic acid leakage of MRSA induced by GLCDs
ce:section.ce:para: A pure strain of MRSA was isolated and subsequently inoculated into MH broth [28]. Following this, the inoculated medium was incubated at 37 °C under gentle agitation until the bacteria attain the mid-logarithmic growth stage. Then, GLCDs powder was weighed and dissolved in PBS. Subsequently, via serial dilution, working solutions of GLCDs with predetermined concentrations of 250, 500, and 750 μg/mL respectively can be obtained. Then, 500 μL of the MRSA (1.0 × 106 CFU/mL) culture was aliquoted into multiple sterile tubes. The previously prepared GLCDs working solutions were added to each tube so as to attain the final concentrations of 250, 500, and 750 μg/mL. The mixtures were then incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. Next, small aliquots were removed from each reaction mixture and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to separate the bacterial cells from the supernatant. The supernatants were collected and the absorbance of nucleic acids at 260 nm were measured using a spectrophotometer, with the aim of quantifying the leaked nucleic acids. Finally, the OD260 values for each sample were recorded and then plotted against time to analyze the kinetics of nucleic acid leakage and assess the impact of GLCDs at the specified concentrations on the integrity of MRSA cell membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental method for detecting ROS in the co-culture System of GLCDs and MRSA using DCFH-DA [29]
ce:section.ce:para: To initiate the process, a pure isolate of MRSA was obtained and then inoculated into MH broth. This inoculated medium was incubated at 37 °C under gentle agitation until the bacteria progress to the mid-logarithmic growth stage. Working solutions of GLCDs with concentrations set at MIC and twice the MIC were acquired. Then, 500 μL of the MRSA culture were dispensed into multiple sterile tubes. The pre-prepared GLCDs working solutions were then added to each tube to attain the final concentrations of MIC and 2MIC. These mixtures were then incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Small aliquots were then extracted and added DCFH-DA (10 mM). The underlying principle here is that DCFH-DA is a cell-permeable, non-fluorescent probe. In the presence of ROS, it undergoes oxidation and is converted into the fluorescent compound 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF). After adding DCFH-DA to the aliquots in strict accordance with the manufacturer's instructions and incubating them briefly to permit the reaction, we can then measure the fluorescence intensity at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm and a detection wavelength of 525 nm. The intensity of the fluorescence detected at 525 nm serves to indirectly quantify the amount of ROS generated, since the greater the presence of ROS, the more DCFH-DA will be oxidized and, consequently, the higher the fluorescence intensity will be. Finally, the recorded fluorescence intensity values for each sample are plotted against GLCDs concentration to analyze the kinetics of ROS generation and evaluate the effect of GLCDs at MIC and twice the MIC on ROS production within the co-culture system involving MRSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition effects of SOD enzyme activity in MRSA by GLCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The experimental procedure for ascertaining the inhibition rate of the intracellular superoxide dismutase (SOD) enzyme activity in MRSA when exposed to GLCDs, by employing the nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT) assay technique, is elaborated as follows [30]. In the initial stage, a pure strain of MRSA was isolated and then inoculated into MH broth. Subsequently, this inoculated medium should be incubated at a controlled temperature of 37 °C under gentle agitation until the bacteria attain the mid-logarithmic growth stage. Following this, the GLCDs need to be weighed and dissolved with the utmost precision in a sterile solvent to formulate stock solutions. Subsequently, through a series of elaborate serial dilutions, a range of working solutions of GLCDs with concentrations spanning from 50 μg/mL to 750 μg/mL can be obtained. Next, 100 μL of the MRSA suspension (1 × 106) are required to be dispensed into 96-well plate. Then, different volumes of the pre-prepared GLCDs working solutions were added to each tube to achieve the final concentrations lying within the 50–750 μg/mL range. Afterward, these mixtures should be incubated at 37 °C for a prearranged and specific time interval. Thereafter, once the incubation is finished, the bacteria have to be harvested by means of centrifugation and the cell membranes disrupted using an appropriate lysis buffer to release the intracellular components, among which the SOD enzyme is of particular interest. Subsequently, the NBT assay is commenced. The underlying principle of this method hinges on the fact that superoxide radicals have the capacity to oxidize NBT, generating formazan that displays a characteristic purple color. In contrast, SOD can hinder this oxidation process, thereby reducing the production of formazan. By measuring the absorbance of the generated formazan, an accurate assessment of the SOD activity level can be made. To carry out the assay, the NBT reagent was added to the samples containing the released intracellular components in strict accordance with the manufacturer's instructions. Then, incubate the reaction mixtures under ideal conditions to promote the reaction. Subsequently, utilize a spectrophotometer to measure the absorbance of the reaction mixtures at 560 nm. It is crucial to note that the absorbance value bears an inverse relationship to the SOD activity. That is, a more active SOD will result in less formazan being produced and, consequently, a lower absorbance reading at 560 nm. Afterward, calculate the inhibition rate of SOD activity for each sample in comparison to the control group (untreated MRSA). The inhibition rate can be computed using the formula:
Inhibition rate

Finally, record the inhibition rate values for each sample and plot them against the GLCDs concentration to analyze the relationship between GLCDs concentration and the inhibition of SOD enzyme activity in MRSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition effects of CAT enzyme activity in MRSA by GLCDs [29]
ce:section.ce:para:
A pure isolate of MRSA bacteria, which had been preserved under optimized storage conditions, was inoculated into a nutrient-enriched medium. Subsequently, the inoculated medium was incubated at a constant temperature of 37 °C under gentle agitation until the bacteria reached the mid-logarithmic growth phase, thereby ensuring the stability and reproducibility of enzymatic activities. Following this, GLCDs were weighed using a high-precision analytical balance and dissolved in a sterile, biocompatible solvent. Through a series of controlled serial dilutions under strict aseptic conditions, a range of working solutions of GLCDs with concentrations spanning from 50 μg/mL to 750 μg/mL were obtained. At the mid-logarithmic growth stage of the MRSA culture, 100 μL was harvested. A pre-optimized buffer solution, designed to mimic the intracellular milieu of MRSA, was added. The cells were then disrupted by a combination of mechanical agitation and enzymatic treatment. After centrifugation at a specific speed and for a predetermined time period, the supernatant, rich in CAT enzyme, was collected and stored at a low temperature to maintain its enzymatic activity. Next, hydrogen peroxide of calibrated concentration was combined with the same buffer in optimized proportions and mixed to formulate the reaction solution. For the construction of the standard curve, a series of hydrogen peroxide solutions with varying concentrations, supplemented with the buffer and CAT enzyme solution obtained from the MRSA lysate, had their absorbance measured at 240 nm using a calibrated spectrophotometer. Multiple replicate measurements were taken to minimize experimental errors. For sample determination, aliquots of the enzyme and reaction solutions were mixed and the absorbance at 240 nm was measured at predetermined time intervals. Based on the principle that CAT enzyme catalyzes the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide, leading to a reduction in absorbance, the CAT activity was calculated from the rate of change of absorbance. Parallel experiments were conducted both in the presence and absence of GLCDs at each concentration. The inhibition rate was calculated using the established formula:
Inhibition rate
%

Finally, the inhibition rate values corresponding to each GLCDs concentration were recorded. Statistical analyses, such as ANOVA analysis, were subsequently employed to dissect the relationship between GLCDs concentration and the inhibition of CAT enzyme activity. The results were presented in a clear, concise, and standardized manner in plot form to facilitate scientific interpretation and dissemination.

The 2,3,5-triphenyltetrazolium chloride (TTC) assay protocol [22] was utilized to explore the impact of GLCDs on MRSA. Briefly, 500 μg/mL GLCDs solution was introduced into the experimental reaction setup that contained an 500 μL of the MRSA bacterial suspension (1 × 106). In parallel, Ginkgo-leaf extract (GLE, 500 μg/mL), a negative control group was established. Both groups were then incubated under 37 °C for 2 h. Then, 10 mL of pre-cooled TTC solution was incorporated into each reaction system and mixed to guarantee intimate contact between TTC and the bacteria. Subsequently, the reaction vessels were transferred to a light-excluded environment for further incubation. The bacteria were capable of metabolically reducing TTC, leading to the formation of red formazan products. The effect of GLCDs on the growth and metabolic dynamics of MRSA bacteria could be visualization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental method and calculation formula for the inhibition rate of GLCDs on the oxidase activity of MRSA
ce:section.ce:para:
Inoculate MRSA into MH broth and incubate at 37°C with shaking at 200 rpm overnight to reach the logarithmic growth phase. Harvest the bacteria by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min and resuspend in PBS (pH 7.4) to an optical density at 600 nm (OD600) of approximately 0.5, corresponding to a cell density of about 1 × 108 colony-forming units per milliliter (CFU/mL). Then, prepare a stock solution of GLCDs with a known concentration. Serial dilutions are then made to obtain different working concentrations, such as 150, 250, 350, 450, 550, 650, 750 μg/mL. Divide the reaction mixtures into two groups: the experimental group and the control group. For the experimental group: Add 100 μL of the bacterial suspension to each well of a 96-well microplate. Then, add 10 μL of different concentrations of GLCDs solutions to the corresponding wells. Incubate the microplate at 37°C for 30 min to allow the interaction between GLCDs and the bacteria. After incubation, add 10 μL of 1 % tetramethyl-p-phenylenediamine dihydrochloride (the oxidase detection reagent) to each well and incubate for another 5 min at room temperature. For the control group: Add 100 μL of the bacterial suspension and 10 μL of PBS (pH 7.4) to the wells. Follow the same procedure as the experimental group by adding the oxidase detection reagent and incubating. Measure the absorbance of each well at 550 nm using a microplate reader. The inhibition rate of GLCDs on the oxidase activity of MRSA (%) is calculated as follows:
Inhibition rate
%

Where Abs

Experimental is the average absorbance value of the experimental group at 550 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment on the disruption of MRSA biofilms by GLCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To begin with, MRSA was incubated overnight in TSB medium, yielding a fresh bacterial suspension. Subsequently, 200 μL of TSB was dispensed into each well of a 96-well sterile plate, and 10 μL of bacteria at a concentration of 106 CFU/mL was inoculated per well. Next, the 96-well plate was transferred to a constant temperature and humidity incubator for a 24-hour incubation period. During this phase, MRSA proliferated continuously, forming a thick, white biofilm at the bottom of the wells. After completion of the cultivation, the medium was aspirated, and the biofilm was washed three times with PBS (pH 7.4). Subsequently, 200 μL of GLCDs TSB solution was introduced into each well, followed by an additional 24-hour static incubation in the incubator. Thereafter, the floating bacteria and GLCDs present in the supernatant were removed, and the biofilm underwent another three washes with PBS (pH 7.4). This paper details three methods for assessing the disruption of biofilms mediated by GLCDs. (1) Crystal violet assay [31]: The disrupted biofilms were dried in an oven set at 60 °C and then fixed with 200 μL of methanol for a duration of 15 min. Once fixed, the methanol was removed, and the biofilms were dried at 60 °C. Subsequently, 100 μL of 0.1 % crystal violet was added to stain the nucleic acids of viable bacteria within the biofilm for 15 min. Subsequently, the excess crystal violet was removed, and the biofilm was washed three times with PBS (pH 7.4). After drying at 60 °C, the biofilm was decolorized with 200 μL of 95 % ethanol in a 37 °C shaking incubator for 30 min. Finally, the absorbance of the mixed solution at 570 nm was measured using a microplate reader. The biofilms that did not receive GLCDs treatment served as the control group, with an inhibition rate assigned as 0%. The absorbance of each well was measured in triplicate and averaged, and the disruption rate of each GLCDs on the biofilm was calculated using the formula:

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observation [32]: The biofilms disrupted by GLCDs were fixed with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde for 24 h and then washed three times with PBS (pH 7.4). Subsequently, gradient elution was carried out using ethanol solutions of 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 80 %, 90 %, 95 %, and 100 %. After supercritical carbon dioxide drying and gold sputtering, the morphological features of the biofilms were visualized using SEM. (3) Optical microscopy observation [33]: The state of the biofilms post-disruption was examined under an optical microscope. The disruption capacity of GLCDs on the biofilms was gauged by observing the density or sparseness of the biofilms. These are the experimental procedures for evaluating the anti-biofilm efficacy of GLCDs.

To investigate the cytotoxicity of GLCDs, CCK-8 assay and cell viability/cytotoxicity staining experiments were carried out employing 3T3 [34]. In the CCK-8 assay protocol, initially, the cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 10⁴ cells per well. Subsequently, GLCDs with concentrations varying from 0 to 1000 μg/mL were introduced into the wells, followed by incubation at 37 °C for a period of 24 h. Post-incubation, the resultant mixture was washed thrice with PBS (pH 7.4) buffer to eliminate the GLCDs. Next, 10 μL of CCK-8 dye and 100 μL of freshly prepared DMEM medium were added to each well, and the plates were then incubated in the dark at 37 °C for 24 h. Eventually, the OD450 was gauged using a microplate reader to appraise the relative survival rate of the viable cells. The untreated cells functioned as the positive control, exhibiting a cell survival rate of 100 %. Each experimental group was replicated three times to derive the average value. Regarding the cell Live/Dead staining experiment, 3T3 cells were likewise utilized [35]. Firstly, fresh DMEM medium supplemented with 10 % serum and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin was readied. Solutions of GLCDs at diverse concentrations within the 0–1024 μg/mL range were added to 96-well plates, with each well containing 200 μL of cell culture medium and 1 × 10⁴ cells. After 24 h of culturing, the medium was removed and the wells were washed twice with PBS (pH 7.4) to expunge the excess medium. Subsequently, 100 μL of a live/dead dye solution (SYTO 9/PI) was added to each well inside a dark chamber at room temperature and further incubated for 15 min. Finally, the cell morphology was scrutinized and fluorescent images were captured via an inverted fluorescence microscope. The control group comprised cells that had not been treated with GLCDs. These are the comprehensive experimental procedures for evaluating the survival rate and Live/Dead staining characteristics of 3T3 cells in the context of GLCDs exposure.

As previously stated [36], hydrogels were fabricated in a 96-well plate and subsequently washed with PBS (pH 7.4). Blood was procured from a healthy volunteer, and the collection process of RBCs adhered to pertinent laws and institutional protocols. RBCs were centrifuged at a relative centrifugal force (RCF) of 1500 g for 5 min and rinsed twice with PBS (pH 7.4). Subsequently, they were suspended in PBS to achieve a 5 % (v/v) suspension. 100 μL of the RBCs suspension was then added either to the surface of the hydrogel or to the blank wells. The RBCs suspension treated with double-distilled water and PBS (pH 7.4) served as the positive control (A
H2O , exhibiting 100 % hemolysis) and the negative control (A
PBS , with 0 % hemolysis), respectively. The RBCs on the hydrogel were incubated at 37 °C for 1 hour. Subsequently, after adding 900 μL of PBS (pH 7.4), the mixture was centrifuged at an RCF of 1500 g for 10 min. 100 μL of the supernatant was transferred to a cuvette, and the absorption value (A
S ) was recorded at 576 nm using an ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer. Hemolytic activity was assessed by quantifying the amount of hemoglobin released into the solution due to cytolysis. The hemolysis rate was computed as [(A
S -A
PBS ) / (A
H2O -A
S ) × 100] %, where A
S represents the absorbance of the hydrogel group. Moreover, following incubation, 10 μL of the RBCs suspension from each group was diluted with 90 μL of PBS (pH 7.4) and transferred to a 96-well plate. The morphology of RBCs was visualized using an Olympus BX51 microscope.

All the values were denoted in the form of Mean ± SD, which were derived from a minimum of three independent and consecutive tests [37]. To assess the significance of the data, the double-tailed heteroscedasticity t-test was employed. This test was calculated via the TTEST function available in Microsoft Excel. A significant difference between groups was deemed to exist when the P value was lower than 0.05.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image ( Fig. 1A) exhibits that GLCDs assume a nearly spherical morphology with a mean diameter of 2.15 nm ± 0.3 nm (standard deviation). In Fig. 1B, the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images manifest the high crystallinity of GLCDs. The lattice spacings of 0.34 and 0.21 nm are attributed to the (002) and (100) planes of hexagonal graphite, respectively [38]. Moreover, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) ring pattern of GLCDs (Fig. 1C) indicates a crystalline carbon phase, where the ratio of the squared ring radii is 3:8:11:16:19, suggesting a diamond-like structure. The rings correspond to the (111), (220), (311), (400), and (331) planes of diamond [39]. Simultaneously, X-ray diffraction (XRD) experiments detected five diffraction peaks at 18.39°, 40.42°, 50.04°, 66.29°, and 73.59°, respectively, further corroborating the crystallization characteristics of GLCDs. The FTIR spectra of GLE and GLCDs are presented in the Fig. E. For GLE, a broad absorption peak at around 3280 cm−1 is observed, which may be attributed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl (-OH) and amino (-NH2) groups, indicating the presence of compounds containing these functional groups in the dried powder of Ginkgo biloba leaf extract [40]. Peaks at approximately 2855 cm−1 and 2927 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of methyl (-CH3) and methylene (-CH2-) groups, respectively, suggesting the existence of organic components with hydrocarbon chains [41]. In the fingerprint region, a series of peaks at 1607 cm−1, 1513 cm−1, 1411 cm−1, 1378 cm−1, 1322 cm−1, 1245 cm−1, 1037 cm−1, 878 cm−1, and 531 cm−1 are present, reflecting the diverse chemical structures of the Ginkgo biloba leaf extract. In contrast, for GLCDs, the overall spectral shape changes significantly compared to GLE. The absorption peak in the region above 3000 cm−1 weakens notably, especially the broad peak at 3280 cm−1 almost disappears, implying possible changes in the functional groups related to hydroxyl and amino groups during the preparation of GLCDs, likely due to structural alterations or content reduction caused by the carbonization process. A new absorption peak appears at 1762 cm−1, which may be attributed to the stretching vibration of carbonyl (CO) groups, indicating the generation of new functional groups containing carbonyl during the formation of GLCDs [42]. In the fingerprint region, peaks at 1603 cm−1 and 1516 cm−1 may still be related to the skeletal vibrations of benzene rings, but with slight shifts in position compared to GLE, suggesting changes in the chemical environment around the benzene rings during the formation of GLCDs. The appearance and changes of peaks at 1762 cm−1, 1406 cm−1, 1319 cm−1, 1071 cm−1, 867 cm−1, and 531 cm−1 indicate significant differences in the chemical structures between GLCDs and GLE, which may be associated with the functional groups on the surface of GLCDs and the vibration modes of the carbon skeleton. XPS spectrum showed O-rich C-dots comprised of 76.87 % C, 1.98 % N, and 21.15 % O (Fig. 1 F). The XPS spectra of GLCDs for C1s, N1s, and O1s are presented in Fig. 1 G∼1I, respectively. For the C1s spectrum (Fig. 1 G), the peak at 284.80 eV is attributed to C-C/C-H bonds, indicating the presence of graphitic carbon structures [43]. The peak at 286.42 eV corresponds to C-N bonds, suggesting the incorporation of nitrogen into the carbon framework [44]. The peak at 288.95 eV is associated with CO/CN bonds, which may imply the existence of carbonyl or imine groups on the surface of GLCDs [45]. The peak at 293.06 eV (π-π*) could be related to the π-π* transitions in aromatic systems, and the peak at 295.84 eV (OC-OH) indicates the presence of carboxyl groups [46]. In the N1s spectrum (Fig. 1H), the peak at 401.37 eV (N-Q) may represent quaternary nitrogen species, which could be incorporated into the graphitic structure [47]. The peak at 400.43 eV (Graphitic N) suggests the presence of nitrogen atoms within the graphitic layers [48]. The peak at 399.73 eV (N-H/NH₂) indicates the existence of amino or amine groups on the surface of GLCDs [49]. Regarding the O1s spectrum (Fig. 1I), the peak at 532.06 eV (O = C) is assigned to carbonyl oxygen, while the peak at 533.08 eV (C-OH/C-O-C) is attributed to hydroxyl or ether oxygen groups [50]. These oxygen-containing functional groups may play important roles in the surface chemistry and properties of GLCDs.

As shown in Fig. 2A, the Zeta potential of GLCDs is determined to be positive (+7.62 mV). This positive value implies that GLCDs may favorably interact with MRSA through electrostatic attraction, thereby facilitating contact and potentially inducing the rupture of the MRSA cell membrane [51]. This property could contribute to the antibacterial effect of GLCDs against MRSA, as electrostatic interaction plays a crucial role in the initial stage of the interaction between nanoparticles and bacterial cells. The UV-Vis spectra of GLE and GLCDs are shown in Fig. 2B. The absorption peak around 280 nm in GLCDs can be attributed to π–π* transitions, likely arising from the conjugated π systems within the GLCDs structure [52]. The absorption near 350 nm is due to n–π* transitions from the surface groups of GLCDs, suggesting the involvement of heteroatoms with lone pairs of electrons interacting with conjugated π systems [53]. The inset images of GLE and GLCDs aqueous solutions under natural light and 360 nm UV light demonstrate that the GLCDs solution emits fluorescence under 360 nm UV irradiation, indicating the presence of fluorescent properties in GLCDs. The fluorescence spectra of GLCDs with excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 500 nm at intervals of 20 nm are depicted in Fig. 2C. It is evident that GLCDs exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent emission, suggesting the existence of multiple energy levels and electronic states within the GLCDs [54]. The observed tailing in the spectra may be attributed to factors such as the heterogeneity of the GLCDs in terms of size, shape, and surface properties, as well as possible energy transfer processes among different chromophores or defect sites within the GLCDs [55]. The 3D fluorescence map of GLCDs in Fig. 3D, with excitation and emission wavelengths ranging from 200 nm to 700 nm, provides a comprehensive visualization of the fluorescence behavior. The contour plot and color scale clearly illustrate the intensity distribution of the fluorescence emission as a function of excitation and emission wavelengths. The region with higher intensity indicates the preferred excitation and emission wavelengths for GLCDs [56], which is consistent with the information obtained from the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 3C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of GLCDs in killing MRSA
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 shows the MICs of GLE, GLCDs, and vancomycin against MRSA. The MIC of GLE is determined to be 4000 μg/mL, indicating a relatively high concentration required for inhibitory activity. In contrast, GLCDs exhibit a significantly lower MIC of 250 μg/mL, suggesting a much higher potency in inhibiting MRSA growth compared to GLE. Vancomycin, as a reference antibiotic, has an MIC of 20 μg/mL. These results imply that GLCDs possess superior antibacterial activity against MRSA in terms of MIC.

Fig.3A illustrates the bactericidal kinetics of GLE against MRSA at concentrations of 4 mg/mL, 8 mg/mL, and 12 mg/mL, respectively. It is evident that as the concentration of GLE increases, the rate and extent of bacterial colony reduction also increase. However, even at the highest concentration of 12 mg/mL, complete elimination of bacteria is not achieved within the tested time frame (240 min). This suggests that although GLE has some antibacterial effect, it is relatively less potent and requires higher concentrations and longer incubation periods to achieve substantial bacterial reduction. Fig. 3B depicts the bactericidal kinetics of GLCDs against MRSA at concentrations of 250 μg/mL, 500 μg/mL, and 750 μg/mL, respectively. Notably, at all tested concentrations of GLCDs, a more pronounced and rapid reduction in bacterial colonies is observed compared to GLE. Even at the lowest concentration of 250 μg/mL, GLCDs demonstrate a significant antibacterial effect, and complete eradication of bacteria is achieved within 240 min at the higher concentrations (500 μg/mL and 750 μg/mL). This further supports the enhanced antibacterial activity of GLCDs against MRSA, as evidenced by both the lower MIC and more effective bactericidal kinetics. It suggests GLCDs′ potential as effective antibacterial agents, warranting further exploration of their mechanism of action and potential applications in combating MRSA infections.

The ONPG assay depicted in Fig. 4A is designed to investigate the potential perturbation of MRSA bacterial cell membranes by GLCDs. For MRSA treated with GLCDs, OD420 values indicate o-nitrophenol (ONP) release progressively increased with extended co-culture time. The absorbance plateaued after 85 min, suggesting the MRSA cell membrane achieved maximal rupture. Conversely, for the control group (GLE-treated MRSA), OD420 values showed no notable changes throughout the culture period, implying no bacterial cell membrane rupture. This indicates GLCDs effectively alter MRSA cell membrane permeability, whereas GLE lacks such an effect. Fig. 4B illustrates the nucleic acid leakage assay, which quantifies the extent of intracellular nucleic acid efflux from MRSA cells upon exposure to varying concentrations of GLE or GLCDs over distinct time intervals. The vertical axis, denoting the OD260 value, is a key indicator, with larger values signifying a greater degree of nucleic acid leakage and, consequently, more pronounced cell membrane damage. It is noteworthy that GLCDs elicit a more significant nucleic acid leakage compared to GLE at equivalent concentrations, thereby suggesting a more potent impact on cell membrane integrity. The ROS detection assay in Fig. 4C focuses on the generation of ROS subsequent to the interaction between GLCDs and MRSA. The peak at 525 nm serves as a reliable indicator of the relative content of ROS. It is evident that GLCDs at the MIC concentration trigger ROS production in MRSA, and this effect is further augmented at a concentration twice the MIC. As shown in Fig. S1, intercellular ROS in MRSA generated by GLCDs is also observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy. In sharp contrast, GLE fails to exhibit a significant increase in ROS upon contact with the bacteria (Fig. 4C). Fig. 4D showcases the inhibition rate of total SOD enzyme activity in MRSA in response to different concentrations of GLE or GLCDs. GLCDs exert a substantial inhibitory effect on SOD enzyme activity, with the inhibition rate escalating as the concentration increases. Conversely, GLE has a negligible impact on SOD enzyme activity. Similar to Fig. 4D, Fig. 4E presents the inhibition rate of CAT enzyme activity in MRSA under the influence of varying concentrations of GLE or GLCDs. GLCDs manifest a notable inhibitory effect on CAT enzyme activity, which intensifies with increasing concentration. In contrast, GLE has a minimal effect on CAT enzyme activity. The TTC assay results in Fig. 4F are presented in the form of photographs. It is observed that MRSA treated with 500 μg/mL of GLE retains high metabolic activity, whereas MRSA treated with the same concentration of GLCDs exhibits a significantly reduced metabolic activity level. This phenomenon may be attributed to the inhibition of respiratory enzyme activity in the mitochondria of MRSA by GLCDs. Fig. 4G investigates the inhibition of cytochrome oxidase, a pivotal respiratory enzyme, by GLCDs. As the concentration of GLCDs increases from 0 to 750 μg/mL, the inhibition of MRSA cytochrome oxidase activity becomes increasingly pronounced. In contrast, an increase in the concentration of GLE from 0 to 750 μg/mL has a negligible effect on cytochrome oxidase activity. Fig. 4H provides further corroboration of the inhibition of cytochrome oxidase activity by GLCDs. As the concentration of GLCDs rises from 0 to 350 μg/mL, the inhibition rate of MRSA cytochrome oxidase activity increases steadily. However, when the concentration exceeds 350 μg/mL, the inhibition rate reaches approximately 80 %, and the trend of increasing inhibition rate becomes less pronounced.

The collective findings from these investigations incontrovertibly demonstrate that GLCDs exert a more significant impact on MRSA relative to GLE. This encompasses membrane perturbation, nucleic acid leakage, ROS generation, inhibition of antioxidant enzymes (SOD and CAT), and inhibition of respiratory enzymes such as cytochrome oxidase. These findings collectively suggest that GLCDs hold potential as an antibacterial agent against MRSA through multiple mechanisms of action. Further research is warranted to elucidate the detailed underlying mechanisms and to explore the potential applications of GLCDs in the context of combating MRSA infections.

In Fig. 5A, a bar graph delineates the destruction rates of mature MRSA biofilms upon exposure to varying concentrations of GLCDs and GLE. A discernible upward trend in the destruction rate of the biofilm is observed as the concentration of GLCDs escalates. Notably, at a concentration of 512 μg/mL, the destruction rate of GLCDs on the biofilm surpasses 98 %, signifying a highly efficacious disruption. In contrast, GLE exhibits a relatively inferior destruction rate across all concentrations, thereby underscoring the superior biofilm-destroying capacity of GLCDs in comparison to GLE. And then, Fig. 5B showcases SEM images of mature MRSA biofilms subsequent to treatment with different concentrations of GLCDs. The PBS control group manifests a dense and intact biofilm architecture. Upon treatment with escalating concentrations of GLCDs, the biofilm undergoes a progressive disruption. As the concentration of GLCDs augments, the biofilm structure becomes increasingly fragmented and less dense. At a concentration of 512 μg/mL, the biofilm is nearly completely obliterated, which aligns with the high destruction rate witnessed in Fig. 5A. These SEM images furnish morphological evidence of the biofilm destruction mediated by GLCDs. Furthermore, Fig. 5C presents optical microscopy images of biofilms stained with crystal violet following treatment with different concentrations of GLCDs. The intensity of the purple hue is inversely correlated with the degree of biofilm destruction, with a deeper purple hue indicating a greater retention of the biofilm and a lighter purple hue suggesting a more pronounced destruction. As the concentration of GLCDs increases, the purple color progressively lightens, indicating an escalating degree of biofilm disruption. This visual observation corroborates the quantitative data from Fig. 5A and the morphological alterations observed in Fig. 5B, confirming the potent biofilm-destroying effect of GLCDs. Finally, Fig. 5D depicts plate cultures of bacteria extracted from MRSA biofilms treated with different concentrations of GLCDs using PBS under ultrasonic treatment. The first plate represents the PBS control group. As the concentration of GLCDs increases, the number of bacterial colonies on the plates exhibits a significant decline. This observation implies that GLCDs not only disrupt the biofilm structure but also curtail the viable bacterial count within the biofilm. The decreasing trend in bacterial colonies with increasing GLCDs concentration suggests a direct correlation between the concentration of GLCDs and the reduction in bacterial viability, further accentuating the potential of GLCDs in combating MRSA biofilm-associated infections [57]. In contrast, at the same concentration, vancomycin shows a less effective disruption of mature MRSA biofilms compared to GLCDs (Fig. S2). This further demonstrates that the GLCDs prepared in this study can serve as an alternative to antibiotics for treating MRSA biofilm infections. Due to the positive surface charge and nano-size effect of GLCDs, they can more easily penetrate the dense biofilm [58].

Fig. 6A shows a bar chart depicting the relative survival rate of 3T3 cells after exposure to varying concentrations of GLCDs for 24 h. It is evident that the relative survival rate of 3T3 cells remains consistently high across all tested concentrations of GLCDs, indicating that GLCDs have minimal cytotoxic effects on 3T3 cells even at high concentrations. Moreover, Fig. 6B presents Live/Dead images of 3T3 cells treated with different concentrations of GLCDs for 24 h. These images visually confirm that GLCDs have essentially no impact on the viability of 3T3 cells. The cell morphology and distribution appear similar across all concentrations, further supporting the results from Fig. 6A. Subsequently, Fig. 6C presents a bar chart illustrating the hemolysis rate of RBCs upon exposure to varying concentrations of GLCDs. The x-axis represents the concentration of GLCDs (μg/mL), ranging from 16 to 1024, while the y-axis represents the hemolysis ratio (%). Notably, the hemolysis ratio remains consistently low across all concentrations of GLCDs tested. Even at the highest concentration of 1024 μg/mL, the hemolysis ratio is minimal, indicating a negligible effect of GLCDs on RBC integrity. Each bar in the chart is accompanied by a photograph of the corresponding concentration of GLCDs co-cultured with RBCs for 1 h. PBS and H₂O serve as the negative and positive controls, respectively. The PBS group shows no visible signs of hemolysis, with the RBC suspension maintaining its typical appearance. In contrast, the H₂O group exhibits complete hemolysis, with the RBCs being lysed and the suspension appearing clear. The visual inspection of the photographs further supports the quantitative data from the bar chart, confirming that GLCDs do not induce significant hemolysis even at high concentrations. What’s more, the inset images in Fig. 6C shows microscopic images of RBCs treated with 1024 μg/mL GLCDs. It can be seen that the RBCs treated with GLCDs retain their biconcave disc shape with a diameter of approximately 7–8 μm, being thinner in the center and thicker at the periphery, which is identical to the morphology of RBCs in the PBS group. In contrast, RBCs treated with H₂O are completely lysed. This further confirms the excellent blood compatibility of GLCDs, which is an essential property for potential biomedical applications involving interaction with blood components. The low hemolysis rates suggest that GLCDs are unlikely to cause adverse hemolytic reactions in vitro, enhancing their safety profile for use in therapeutic or diagnostic contexts where blood contact may occur [59].

In summary, we successfully synthesized nitrogen self-doped GLCDs via a one-step solvothermal method and extraction technology. The GLCDs possess unique properties, such as amphiphilicity, a spherical crystalline form, and a positive charge, endowing them with potent antibacterial activity against MRSA. Their low MIC and rapid elimination of high bacterial loads, along with a dose-dependent inhibition of MRSA biofilm formation, are significant advantages. Our investigation into the antimicrobial mechanisms of GLCDs against MRSA revealed multiple pathways. Electrostatic interaction facilitates penetration, followed by cell structure disruption and intracellular component leakage. Oxidative stress induction and inhibition of antioxidant enzymes and respiratory function also contribute to their antibacterial efficacy. Importantly, GLCDs exhibited high biocompatibility in hemolysis and cytotoxicity assays, indicating their potential for biomedical applications. This study provides a novel antimicrobial nanomaterial from natural plants and offers insights for the rational use of natural biomass against MRSA. Future research should focus on optimizing GLCDs and conducting in vivo studies to evaluate their efficacy and safety in complex biological systems, with the aim of addressing the threat of MDR bacteria and improving public health.

Zhao Gan: Data curation. Li Peili: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Chen Junhua: Project administration. Wei Jumeng: Project administration. Meng Long: Data curation. Li Zirong: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. ke Xiang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhang Da: Project administration. Xu Yunhe: Project administration.
